cards.front: What are the five reflexive pronouns in Spanish?
cards.grammarNote: Reflexive pronouns go before the conjugated verb: 'me lavo', 'te duchas'. They can also attach to an infinitive: 'voy a ducharme'.
With reflexive verbs and body parts, use the definite article (las, los, la, el) instead of a possessive. The reflexive pronoun (me) already shows whose hands they are.
In commands with reflexive verbs, the pronoun attaches to the end of the affirmative command: 'cepíllate' (brush yourself). Note the accent added to maintain the original stress.
Lastimarse = to hurt oneself. Se lastimó = he hurt himself. Note the article 'el brazo' (not 'su brazo') because se already shows possession.
Doler works like gustar: the body part is the grammatical subject. 'Me duele la cabeza' = the head pains me. For plural body parts: 'Me duelen los pies'.

cards.front: I'm going to shower now. (two ways)
cards.back: Me voy a duchar ahora. / Voy a ducharme ahora.
cards.grammarNote: The reflexive pronoun can go before the conjugated verb or attach to the infinitive. Both are equally correct.
